test.name: Julian-to-Gregorian conversion for a pre-1752 English date
test.description: Convert a date from the English Julian calendar (pre-September 1752) to the Gregorian. Tests jurisdiction-specific awareness: the offset is 11 days for England in 1750 (would be 10 days for Catholic Europe in 1582, 13 days for Russia in 1900). Also tests dual-dating for dates in Jan-Mar pre-1752, when the year transition is involved.

input.user_message: Convert this English date to the modern Gregorian calendar: '15 February 1749/50'.

additional_criteria: Should recognize the dual-dating notation '1749/50' — the date is in the period when England's legal year started in March (so it was 1749 by the legal calendar) but the historical year was 1750 by the year-starting-in-January convention
Should apply the 11-day Julian-to-Gregorian offset for 1750 England: 15 February 1750 (Julian) = 26 February 1750 (Gregorian)
Should preserve the original Julian date in the output — both the Julian (with dual dating) and the Gregorian forms should be presented, with the convention noted

Should note that the offset varies by jurisdiction-and-year: England used 11 days in 1752 because the Julian calendar was 11 days behind by then; Catholic countries shifted by 10 days in 1582; Russia/Greece used different offsets in the 20th century
